aliases: Chicken with Black Bean Sauce and Rice

english: Chicken with Black Bean Sauce and Rice

ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed dishes with meat/poultry/fish/seafood and starch

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Black Beans Sauce Chicken Rice is approximately 450 grams, presented as a plate of steamed white rice topped with stir-fried chicken pieces in black bean sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken with sauce and rice, mixed dish (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Black Beans Sauce Chicken Rice exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from the input record (originally sourced from GrabFood menu data) and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked chicken (FDC ID 171477), cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), and black bean sauce ingredients. The input values are plausible for a dish of this composition. GrabFood is a forbidden primary source; USDA FoodData Central is cited as the approved reference anchor for this adaptation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (average GI 64, University of Sydney GI Database), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. The presence of protein from chicken and fat from black bean sauce may modestly attenuate postprandial glucose response, but white rice remains the primary glycaemic driver. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 64.98) / 100 ≈ 41.3, where carbohydrate per 450g serving = 14.44 × 4.5 = 64.98g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 400mg per 100g, equivalent to 1800mg per 450g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content from fermented black beans, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or low-sodium condiment substitutes is strongly advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrates from white rice with medium GI of 64 and low dietary fibre at 0.67g per 100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in moderation, substituting white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycaemic load, and increasing the proportion of chicken and non-starchy vegetables to improve the carbohydrate-to-fibre ratio.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (400mg/100g) with moderate saturated fat (1.56g/100g) and trace trans fat (0.04g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish due to its high sodium content, which promotes fluid retention and elevated blood pressure; choosing reduced-sodium preparation and smaller portion sizes will help mitigate cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Black Beans Sauce Chicken Rice is a popular Asian mixed dish consisting of tender stir-fried chicken pieces coated in a rich, deeply savoury fermented black bean sauce, served alongside a portion of steamed white rice. The sauce is typically prepared with fermented black beans (dou chi), garlic, ginger, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil, producing a complex umami flavour with mild saltiness and a slightly glossy texture. The chicken is succulent and absorbs the sauce well, while the steamed rice provides a neutral, starchy base. Nutritionally, the dish delivers approximately 151 kcal per 100g, with 10g protein, 6.2g fat, and 14.4g carbohydrate. Sodium is notably high at 400mg per 100g, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The white rice base contributes a medium glycaemic index of approximately 64, which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is commonly found in Singaporean Chinese hawker centres and casual dining establishments, and is a staple comfort food across Southeast Asia.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values originate from the input record (GrabFood, a forbidden primary source) and have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked chicken and white rice. The values are plausible for a dish of this composition but cannot be confirmed as exact without a direct approved-source entry for this specific dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Potassium estimated at 160mg/100g based on typical chicken and rice dish composition cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central component data. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific mixed dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg set to -1: detailed vitamin micronutrient data for this specific dish are not reported in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo for an equivalent mixed dish entry as of 2024. phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: mineral micronutrient data beyond calcium and iron are not available in approved sources for this specific dish preparation as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific mixed dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Foodo as of 2024. The input cultural metadata (primary_cuisine: Filipino, ethnic_tags including Noodle/Coffee/Cake) appeared erroneous for this dish and has been corrected to Chinese cuisine with Singapore as region of origin.
